data.body:
## Context
DSO's planning pipeline (brainstorm → preplanning → implementation-plan) is optimized for feature development — building new things where scope is defined by the spec. When the scope is instead defined by the codebase (removal, migration, cross-cutting refactors), the pipeline produces incomplete plans. A recent v2 ticket system cleanup epic passed through the full pipeline and emerged with 11 missing source files, 7+ tests that would break, and success criteria that covered only 3 of 5 file categories the user explicitly requested. The root cause chain: explore agents produced an incomplete inventory, brainstorm didn't catch the gap between user request and spec, the evaluator misrouted the epic as MODERATE despite 51 files across 5 layers, and implementation-plan drafted tasks without analyzing which existing tests would break.

## Success Criteria
1. The complexity evaluator uses a blast-radius scoring function with cross-stack structural signals (path depth, known config/entry/wiring file names, directory conventions, layer count, change type) to force COMPLEX routing when the score exceeds a threshold — signals validated across at least 4 tech stacks
2. The Explore agent prompt template produces structured, enumerated output with a completeness self-check, and explicitly matches all terms from the exploration request
3. Brainstorm's gap analysis detects when success criteria omit artifacts the user explicitly named in their request, and surfaces qualitative completeness questions before the fidelity review
4. Implementation-plan independently enumerates affected files and their associated tests, and produces tasks that modify existing tests for changed behavior, remove tests for deleted behavior, and create tests for new behavior — reinforcing TDD as behavioral specification, not just write new RED tests
5. The existing test suite passes after all changes (validate.sh --ci)

## Dependencies
Epic bbba-8154 (Planning Resilience) — depends_on

## Approach
Five improvements scoped to where they are most effective: blast-radius scoring in the complexity evaluator only (cross-stack signals detectable with Glob — no AST or language-specific tooling); contradiction detection in brainstorm (comparing user named artifacts against SCs — no file enumeration); file impact enumeration and bidirectional test analysis in implementation-plan (concrete file list available at task-drafting time); and explore agent prompt hardening (structured output with completeness self-check). Each improvement is independently testable.

## Research Context (session 2026-03-25)
- MCP sub-agent pattern is broken (github.com/anthropics/claude-code issues #25200, #13898, #29655). MCP research deferred to epic dso-soyt.
- Cross-stack blast radius signals validated: path depth, known config names (~40 patterns), directory conventions (config/, utils/, shared/, lib/, pkg/), entry/wiring file names (main.*, routes.*, app.*, index.{ts,js}, lib.rs). Validated across Go (kubernetes), TypeScript (next.js), Rust (tokio), Java (spring-petclinic), Ruby (discourse).
- Import fan-in detection dropped (requires AST parsing). Framework-specific wiring detection dropped (requires annotation parsing). Safeguard file lists dropped (project-specific).
